scenario: Granite Bank pulls every transaction reference that begins with 12 from a free-text memo for 8800 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a service task input mapping must apply the extract function on the literal references are 1234, 1256, 1378 with the pattern 12 followed by zero or more digits.
question: What does the FEEL extract function return for an input that has multiple regex matches in the input string?

options.text: It returns only the first match of the regex pattern in the input string and discards any further occurrences that appear later in the input text
It returns the input string with the matched substrings highlighted by surrounding marker characters so the worker can read which segments were detected
It returns a list of every match of the regex pattern in the input string, or an empty list when the pattern does not match anywhere in the input
It returns a boolean indicating whether the regex pattern matches at least once anywhere inside the input string and not the matched characters themselves

a.text: Incorrect. Extract returns all matches of the pattern across the input rather than only the first one, as illustrated by the example returning 1234 and 1256.

b.text: Incorrect. The function does not modify the input or add highlighting characters; it returns a separate list of matched substrings.

c.text: Correct. Extract returns a list of strings containing all matches of the regular expression pattern in the input and returns an empty list when no match is found, in line with the string functions reference.

d.text: Incorrect. A boolean check is the role of matches; extract returns the matched substrings rather than a true or false value.

explanation: FEEL extract takes an input string and a regular expression and returns a list of every substring that matches the pattern, or an empty list when no match is found.
